title: Agilent Technologies, Inc. (A) Intrinsic Value
description: An independent discounted cash flow (DCF) intrinsic value analysis of Agilent Technologies (A) compiled entirely by Gemini 3.1.

fcfGrowth.title: FCF Growth Rate (Y1-Y5)
fcfGrowth.rationale: An 8.0% growth rate assumes Agilent continues to capitalize on its high-margin recurring revenue streams (consumables and services) while navigating cyclical capital expenditure trends in the broader laboratory market.

discountRate.title: Discount Rate (WACC)
discountRate.rationale: An 8.5% discount rate reflects Agilent's stable, diverse revenue base and strong balance sheet, offset slightly by its exposure to global macroeconomic cycles and capital spending fluctuations.

terminalGrowth.title: Terminal Growth Rate
terminalGrowth.rationale: A 3.0% terminal rate is appropriate for a mature, industry-leading provider of mission-critical scientific tools, aligning with long-term economic expansion.

thesisTitle: The "Razor and Blades" Advantage in Laboratories
thesisParagraphs: Agilent Technologies has successfully transitioned from its HP origins into a powerhouse within the analytical laboratory ecosystem. With roughly $4.4 billion in recent revenue, its core strength lies in its "razor and blades" business model. While selling high-end instrumentation (the razor) is subject to capital expenditure cycles, the real value driver is the recurring stream of consumables, software licenses, and service contracts (the blades) required to keep those labs operational.

This installed base creates immense switching costs. Laboratories are highly reluctant to retrain staff and revalidate methods on competitor equipment. This stickiness affords Agilent pricing power and margin resilience. The DCF analysis suggests the market is underestimating Agilent's ability to generate compounding free cash flows over the next decade, making it a compelling, undervalued prospect.
verdictFooter: Based on recent financial actuals. Data via Gemini 3.1 (Mar 20, 2026).

faq.question: What is driving Agilent's free cash flow growth?
faq.answer: Growth is driven by a strategic shift toward higher-margin recurring revenues. As the installed base of instruments grows, the corresponding demand for high-margin consumables and services increases structurally.

faq.question: Is an 8% FCF growth rate achievable?
faq.answer: Yes. Agilent's diverse end-markets (ranging from pharma to environmental testing) mitigate sector-specific downturns, while secular trends in global testing standards support mid-to-high single-digit long-term growth.

faq.question: Why is Agilent currently undervalued?
faq.answer: The market occasionally over-penalizes Agilent for short-term softness in capital equipment orders, underappreciating the stability and cash generation of its underlying recurring revenue model.
